title: Add route planning mode with weather-aware ETA and per-leg risk warnings

plan.summary:
Add a Route Planner mode to the existing Leaflet sailing app. The user toggles planning on, clicks the map to drop ordered waypoints (the boat's water-snapped position is the route origin), and gets a polyline route with per-leg distance, bearing, and an estimated time based on a configurable boat speed — plus total distance, total time, and an ETA clock. Each leg is made weather-aware: the backend fetches the forecast at the leg's destination for the hour matching that leg's estimated arrival time and flags simple risks: 'headwind', 'low visibility', 'pressure drop nearby', and 'arrival after sunset'. The existing wind/waves/radar/cloud/pressure overlays stay available and overlaid in route mode (these are global layers; nothing about them changes). The work follows the app's established patterns exactly: a thin handler delegating to pure, unit-testable functions (geometry + warnings) plus a fetch function in the Result style ({ok,data}/{ok,error}); a new draggable floating panel consistent with the other controls; and CSS/HTML kept out of handlers. The heavy client logic is added as a self-contained module appended to the JS bundle in _temp_build (id=1), matching how prior features (water detection, overlays, draggable panels) were added.

Why this approach: geometry (distance/bearing/ETA) is computed server-side in a pure function so it is directly testable and so the same call can attach weather warnings in one coherent response; warnings live in a second pure function fed already-resolved numbers, so timezone/forecast-hour resolution (the messy part, tied to the Open-Meteo response shape) is isolated in the orchestrator and covered by an integration test. A dedicated fetchRouteWeather (one request per leg, hourly winds/precip/pressure/cloud + daily sunrise/sunset, no marine call) is used instead of touching the hot shared fetchWeather (which analyzeDirections calls up to 8x) — keeping the shared function and its tests untouched. Tradeoff: route weather costs one API call per leg, so the number of waypoints is capped by config (ROUTE_MAX_WAYPOINTS) and fetches are batched to respect Open-Meteo rate limits.

steps.description: Step 1 (reqs 3,5,6): Add app configs via set_config (module 'route'): ROUTE_DEFAULT_SPEED_KNOTS=5, ROUTE_MAX_WAYPOINTS=12, ROUTE_HEADWIND_ANGLE_DEG=60, ROUTE_HEADWIND_MIN_KNOTS=10, ROUTE_LOW_VIS_PRECIP_MM=2, ROUTE_LOW_VIS_CLOUD_PCT=95, ROUTE_PRESSURE_LOW_HPA=1005, ROUTE_PRESSURE_DROP_HPA=3 — each with a clear description so the warning thresholds and boat speed default are tunable.

Step 2 (reqs 2,3): Define pure function computeRoutePlan(points, speedKnots, startTimeMs) in module 'route'. Haversine distance in nautical miles (R=3440.065nm) and initial great-circle bearing per leg, 16-point compass label, durationHours = distanceNm/speed, cumulative depart/arrive UTC ms from startTimeMs. Returns { legs:[{index,from,to,distanceNm,bearing,bearingCompass,durationHours,departTimeMs,arriveTimeMs}], totals:{distanceNm,durationHours,departTimeMs,etaMs} }; returns null for <2 points or speed<=0.

Step 3 (req 6): Define pure function computeLegWarnings(bearing, hourData, prevHourData, arriveUtcMs, sunsetUtcMs, sunriseUtcMs, thresholds) in module 'route'. Flags: headwind (wind FROM within headwindAngleDeg of the leg bearing AND windSpeed>=headwindMinKnots), low visibility (precipitation>=lowVisPrecipMm OR cloudCover>=lowVisCloudPct), pressure drop nearby (pressure<=pressureLowHpa OR pressure fell >=pressureDropHpa vs prevHourData), arrival after sunset (arriveUtcMs>=sunsetUtcMs or <=sunriseUtcMs when known). Returns { warnings:[{code,label}], riskLevel:'ok'|'caution'|'warning' } (0/1/>=2 warnings).

Step 4 (req 6): Define function fetchRouteWeather(lat, lon) in module 'route' (Result pattern). One Open-Meteo request: hourly wind_speed_10m/wind_direction_10m/wind_gusts_10m/precipitation/pressure_msl/cloud_cover (m/s→knots), daily sunrise,sunset, forecast_hours=DEFAULT_FORECAST_HOURS, forecast_days=3, timezone=auto; returns { hourly[], daily{time,sunrise,sunset}, timezone, utcOffsetSeconds }. Separate from the shared fetchWeather to avoid changing the directions hot-path; duplication justified.

Step 5 (reqs 2,3,4,6): Define orchestrator function planRoute(points, speedKnots, startTimeMs, thresholds, maxWaypoints) in module 'route' (Result pattern). Validates point count vs maxWaypoints, runs computeRoutePlan, fetches each leg destination via fetchRouteWeather in small batches (rate-limit friendly), resolves the forecast hour nearest each arrival and the destination's sunrise/sunset for the arrival's local date using utcOffsetSeconds, calls computeLegWarnings, and attaches conditions+warnings+riskLevel per leg. Returns { speed, startTime, totals, legs }.

Step 6 (reqs 1,2,3,4,6): Define handler getRoutePlan(c) in module 'route' (POST). Validates the JSON body (points array with numeric in-range lat/lon, length 2..max+1; speed numeric>0 defaulting to ROUTE_DEFAULT_SPEED_KNOTS; optional startTime), reads the 8 route configs into a thresholds object, calls planRoute, and maps Result to HTTP (200 data / 400 validation / 502 weather failure). Register route POST /api/route-plan with a body+response schema (module 'route').

Step 7 (reqs 1,2,3,4,5,7,10): Add the client route module to the JS bundle (_temp_build id=1) as a self-contained block: state (routeMode, routeWaypoints, routeLayer, routePlan, routeBoatSpeed); map-click branch (add waypoint in route mode, else move boat as today); addRouteWaypoint/renderRoute (numbered markers + per-leg polylines colored by riskLevel + midpoint distance/bearing labels); recomputeRoute (POST /api/route-plan with [boat,...waypoints], speed, startTime=now; debounced); renderRoutePanel (mode toggle, speed input, empty-state hint, leg list with warning badges, totals + ETA, undo/clear); setupRouteControls; toggleRouteMode/undoRouteWaypoint/clearRoute. Add 'route-panel' to DRAGGABLE_IDS, recompute when the boat (origin) moves, and call setupRouteControls() in boot. Verify served JS via /api/sailing-js.

Step 8 (reqs 1,5,7,10): Add #route-panel (a draggable .panel with drag-handle, top-center) to the sailing-app template and a new sailing-route.css asset (panel, toggle button, speed input, leg rows, color-coded warning badges, totals/ETA, empty state, waypoint markers, leg labels); link the stylesheet in <head>.

Step 9 (all reqs): Tests + checks. Add define_test: testComputeRoutePlan (geometry/ETA + speed scaling + null guards), testComputeLegWarnings (headwind, low-vis, pressure-drop, after-sunset, clear cases + riskLevel), testRoutePlanEndpoint (POST real Mediterranean coords → legs/totals/warnings shape), testRoutePlanValidation (missing/too-few/out-of-range points and bad speed → 400). Update testServeApp (route-panel + sailing-route.css link + drag-handle count now 7) and testSailingJsEndpoint (route functions present, DRAGGABLE_IDS includes 'route-panel'). Run run_tests, check_export_types, and test_coverage; fix until clean; screenshot the route flow.

Assumptions: (1) The boat's current water-snapped position is the route origin; waypoints are kept exactly where clicked (not water-snapped) for predictability. (2) 'current' in the request refers to keeping the existing wind/waves/radar/cloud/pressure overlays available in route mode — the app has no ocean-current dataset, so none is fabricated. (3) ETA is start time (now) + cumulative leg durations at constant boat speed; ETA clock is shown in the viewer's local time while the per-leg 'after sunset' check uses each destination's own timezone. (4) POST is used for /api/route-plan because the ordered waypoint list is structured data. (5) Waypoint count is capped (ROUTE_MAX_WAYPOINTS) to bound per-leg weather API calls.

acceptanceCriteria.text: Route planning mode is available in the sailing map, uses the boat position as the route origin, and lets the user add ordered waypoints by clicking the map.
Each route leg reports distance, bearing, duration, cumulative totals, and ETA using the configured boat speed.
Each leg is evaluated against forecast conditions at its estimated arrival hour and flags headwind, low visibility, pressure drop nearby, and arrival after sunset.
The route planning API validates point count, coordinate ranges, waypoint limits, and boat speed before returning a route plan.
The frontend includes a draggable route panel with planning toggle, speed input, undo/clear controls, waypoint rendering, color-coded leg risks, warning badges, totals, and ETA.
Existing wind, waves, radar, cloud, and pressure overlays remain available while route planning is active.
Boat speed default, waypoint cap, and all route warning thresholds are exposed as route configuration values.

verificationPlan.description: Run `testComputeRoutePlan` to verify haversine distance, bearing/compass labels, cumulative depart/arrival times, speed scaling, totals, ETA, and null guards.
Run `testComputeLegWarnings` to cover headwind, low visibility, pressure drop, after-sunset, clear cases, and risk-level tiers.
Run `testRoutePlanEndpoint` and `testRoutePlanValidation` against the registered `POST /api/route-plan` route.
Run updated served-app and sailing-JS endpoint tests to confirm `#route-panel`, `sailing-route.css`, route functions, and `DRAGGABLE_IDS` wiring are present.
Run export type checks and route-module coverage to verify the route module is clean and fully covered.
Exercise the live browser route flow and verify click-to-server-to-render behavior with no console errors.

verificationRuns.summary: Backfilled from the completed work log: 39/39 tests passed, export type check was clean, route-module coverage was 100%, and a live browser test confirmed the route click-to-server-to-render loop with no console errors.

evidence.summary: 39/39 tests passed, including new route tests for computeRoutePlan geometry/ETA/speed scaling/null guards, computeLegWarnings warning/risk tiers, route endpoint behavior, and validation failures.
`POST /api/route-plan` was covered with real Mediterranean coordinates plus missing, too-few, out-of-range, and bad-speed validation cases.
Served app and JS endpoint tests confirmed `#route-panel`, `sailing-route.css`, route client functions, and `route-panel` in `DRAGGABLE_IDS`.
Export type check was clean and route-module coverage reported 100%.
A live browser test confirmed the route planning click-to-server-to-render loop and reported no console errors.

residualRisks: No ocean-current overlay was added because the project has no ocean-current data source; existing wind, waves, radar, cloud, and pressure overlays were kept available in route mode.
